and-

... there's a bit more to come on that, uh, I think tomorrow.

health. Um, exports of food and fiber products, um, remain
strong despite, uh, some trade turbulence.
And we will release our situation outlook for primary industries in the
next week or two. And, um, you know, I expect us to build on the excellent,

So, look, I'm really proud-
(clears throat)
... of the work that the sector has done across the last year....
happens in a lot of ways, um, through supporting the work of catchment groups on
the ground, backing research to help reduce emissions, supporting farms and growers
to recover from adverse events, uh, market access work of course, and
by maintaining some of the strongest biosecurity measures, uh, in the world.
And of course, not forgetting the sustainability of our seafood sector and, and of
course our important forestry sector.
Uh, as part of our focus on growing, uh, New Zealand's economy, um, in the last
two months, I have visited five of our top six trading
nations. Um, so I've been in Australia, United
States, Japan, Korea, and China, um, with a focus
on maintaining, um, the good relationships that we have there and opening up
market access opportunities wherever we can.
Um, and we have good access in overseas markets, um, but there are also
opportunities for investment back into New Zealand.
And some of the conversation around growing our agriculture sector,
uh, is a real opportunity and one where we're, we are, are
talking about that internationally in order to try and bring, uh, interest and
investment, uh, for those very high value salmon, mussel and oyster products
to see if we can grow that sector out.
Um, certainly for me, um, our reputation's strong
internationally. People like our products, they trust where they come from, uh, and
how we produce them. Uh, and we're in a pretty, uh, positive position, albeit that
there's a lot of competition, uh, particularly from our, our cousins
across, across the ditch.
I also got to visit some of the cutting edge sites and the investments that we have
in methane mitigating technologies, uh, while I was in the United
States. I particularly wanted to see that, um, and happy to talk
about, uh, what that was looking like and what I think the opportunities might be
there. And in Japan, it was good to be able to visit the, you know...
New Zealand imports a large amount of, uh, secondhand vehicles from
Japan, just so happens they drive on the same side of the road as us.
So, um, that's, that's a great export,
uh, to New Zealand. But all of those go through treatment facilities before they
come to New Zealand for biosecurity.
And I was able to go through that facility myself and have a look at how they do
it. Actually found a stink bug on the floor that had dropped out of the heat
treatment chambers, so they do work.
Um, it's quite an important part of the operation.
Obviously those cars are checked again when they arrive in New Zealand, but it's a,
it's a, it's quite a big part of the operation, so it was good to be able to see
that. Um, we made a big investment on this last
year in getting, uh, lifting our plant health and environment laboratory.
And I'd encourage you if you get a chance when you're in Auckland, take a look.
It's next to the pla- uh, the old plant and food laboratory.
Um, it's coming out of the ground quite quickly.
It's on, it's on track and it will give us, um, the capability as the
years go by to be able to manage any large incursions that might
affect our, our, our, um, our plant food industry,
uh, or our forestry industry. But also it's increasing the capacity for us to bring
in new varietals and things, which has always been a bit of a bottleneck, uh, to
bring those. If you bring a new varietal into the country, it might be a prunus
type thing, it can be a couple of years in a quarantine facility before we can be
certain that there are no viruses or diseases that are present.
It's always a bit challenging that area, but we're, we're, we're growing out the
capacity. Our, our facility in Tamaki that looks after plant health is end of
life. Um, and some of those pa- some of the parts of that facility have started to
break down. So we're right on track for getting a new facility 2029, uh, that's
happening now. So we were pretty pleased to get that investment.
It's a substantial investment. Um, it's, it's about, um, 600 odd
million, um, for the development of that.
But it has a 50 year lifespan and that will give us alongside our animal and plant,
our animal health facility out at Wallsville, uh, the one in, the one in Auckland
will cover off both sides, uh, of the equation.
So we'll have animal health and plant health, um, with facilities that guarantee,
uh, we can do the things we need to do in New Zealand.
Um, look, there's obviously been some, uh, challenges, some ups and downs with
weather and other adverse events across the last year, whether it was kind of not
enough rain or too much, uh, or too much wind and those types of things.
There's, there's always been challenges there.
Uh, and of course we successfully dealt with the outbreaks of bird flu in
Otago, although it had a bit of a tail on it in terms of, uh, trade related
matters across the past, uh, year, which we've managed to largely,
uh, restore. We had two fruit fly incursions,
um, uh, last summer. Uh, so we were quite busy through that
Christmas period and of course our team's pretty busy with the hornet at the
moment, uh, in Auckland. So, um, just a bit of an update on the hornet, um, Stu
will be able to give you a bit more on it later, but, um, we've caught 31 queens.
Um, so mo- 20 of those have nests or there's evidence they're nesting.
Um, uh, so that's, that's been good.
Most of these catches have been through visual inspections-
Mm-hmm.
... um, rather than reported, um, sightings.
Four and a half thousand notifications.
So we've kind of whittled that down to the ones that we've f- found that are
actually accurate. And over 2,200 houses visited,
um, in that, um, uh, affected area.
We're starting a leaflet drop at the moment, which will go to a hundred thousand
houses in that North Shore area and that we've extended the buffer zone out to five
Ks. And, uh, we are just wanting to make sure if we can just
get every householder having a bit of a look around the eaves and the decks
and you know, if you've got a wood pile or, um, some particularly,
um, uh, interesting trees and so on in your backyard, if you
can find these little golf ball or tennis size ball, um, nests
and let us know. And that's gonna be our best way, uh, of being able to, of being
able to kind of close this down. Um, so look, we're in a, we've, we've started
well. Um, it's not, it's not, it's not cheap either.
And so just to give you a bit of a sense, you know, we've probably spent around
about a million dollars already on this response.
Um, uh, and we have to be conscious of following the
science in order to kind of spend that money wisely as we go forward.
'Cause each month tick by, ticks by, if you're adding on at roughly a million
dollars a month, uh, in response costs with a lot of people on the ground, it
starts to become very expensive. So we just need to make sure that, um, we're
spending the money upfront, um, in order to try and close this down.
But, you know, we will have to keep making choices about how far we go and how much
we do along the way. So that'll, might be a point of interest for the committee
along the course of next year as well, depending on whether we can close it out
early or whether this kind of starts to, to take a slightly longer course.
Um, probably,
um-... uh, one other thing I did, a couple of things I wanted to mention.
M. bovis, um, M- Minister Hogg had mentioned it, uh, earlier
this week. Uh, this, this is a su- this is really a subst- really substantive
point in our progress on this programme.
And we have spent nearly $800 million on it so far of the $860 million, uh, that
was, that was voted for it. Um, we always thought it was a 10-year
programme. It's likely that we'll be able to conclude it a year earlier than that.
So, where we are right now, uh, sort of seven to, to eight years
into the programme, um, we, we haven't seen the disease
for two years, so we've, we've been able to move to this, um,
confidence phase of absence of the disease.
If we get through one more spring season, autumn and spring calving season,
so this time next year, if we don't see the disease-
(clears throat)
... turn up, we think a year earlier than anticipated, we'd be able to declare
freedom from the disease.
Excellent.
It's substantial because so many people were affected.
Um, hundreds of farmers, uh, who lost, um, who had their cows
culled. Many other, probably thousands of farmers who had notices of direction
at various points throughout the process as we tried to understand what was
happening with their herds. Uh, and, you know, people have been,
um, incredibly good through that. It's not been easy.
I mean, we were a bit bumpy at the start, as you can imagine.
Um, we had nobody dedicated to this and then we had 450 staff on
it, uh, for a long period. And, you know, finding the expertise, the
capability, and building our systems.
We have learnt a lot as a result of d- undertaking this.
A, in terms of, uh, how to ma- how to help
support people and to get in early with those supports.
And, and B, to have the capability to know how to respond to these animal
diseases. It's the biggest one we've tried to get in front of, uh, in
the cattle sector, um, short of TB, which kind of really kicked off, uh, big
time in the '70s and '80s. So, um, we're almost there.
Uh, we're not home yet. Um, we should feel proud about it.
And, you know, what it means for New Zealand, uh, New Zealand, New Zealand farming
systems move their animals around more than almost any other in the world.
Mm-hmm.
That's how we're so productive. And so, uh, if you're moving animals around a
lot, you, you run the risk of spreading disease quite rapidly.
So, to maintain the farming system we had, removing this disease was important to
New Zealand. Uh, we are only one of two countries in the world that doesn't have
the disease. Uh, Norway being the other one.
Uh, but avoiding lameness, mastitis, uh, and aborted
calves, and, uh, farmers having to be much more
discerning about where their animals come from in the future, uh, is something
we've managed to avoid. So, uh, almost there.
Uh, and just did want to acknowledge that a huge amount of work has been done in
that area. Um, last few points. Um, we
have 121 performance measures. Um, it's a big department.
Um, not so much, uh, you know, it's 3,250 staff.
It's not so much the size staffing-wise, but the breadth of what we
cover. Uh, we've achieved 107 of those 121
performance measures, about 88%.
So, we were about 89% last year, so we're roughly in the same place.
Um, but I would note that seven of them weren't active, i.e.
we didn't have to activate those measures because we didn't have to administer
adverse events funds and things like that, uh, in particular, well, particular
programmes to h- of recovery. Uh, so we were probably a little bit
better than, than we showed on the performance there.
But that's, that's all detailed out.
And I did want to acknowledge that, that we've taken a pretty strong disciplined
approach to our financial management across the last year or two in particular.
Um, we were able to increase our capital investment, uh, in MPI's critical
systems and assets. So sometimes these things don't get seen, but they're actually
really important. If we're managing the place well, we can keep investing.
Um, so that Plant Health Laboratory is a big investment, um, ahead of
getting the government's investment, um, uh, capital investment, we, we put in
nearly $20 million of our own investment to get to the point of having a business
case that would support that development.
Um, digitizing the trade certification.
So this is kind of getting, if you think about export certificates for all of the
products that leave New Zealand, moving from paper to digitization.
And with China, uh, we've just agreed to go further with that to almost get to
100%. Um, so we are leading with them in the world at digitizing
trade. Uh, our upgrade of the ETS forestry system
and, um, the Cameras on Boats programme, uh, there's been a big investment.
Uh, opening of, well, I guess I'd say they're not new, but, um, two international
airports reopened, Hamilton and Dunedin.
And so we had-
Oh. ... to staff those, uh, made sure the facilities were okay.
So Dunedin was a bit of a refresh. Hamilton, we had to, uh, work with the airport
companies and get the investment right and so on.
And I think you've had some of those folks through here.
Uh, a new mail center was opened with New Zealand Post and Customs.
(clears throat)

Worth having a look at if you, if you want to have a, have a, a view of that.
Just seeing the, um, 3D technology that we're able to use
for looking at parcels and things to see what's inside them.
So we're in a much stronger position there.
And some of that technology-

It's taken years to get to a point where we got the investment, where we completed
the business case. That business case has been through multiple iterations, uh, and
through cabinet. Uh, we've had to prove that, um, this is the right thing to
do. And, um, and I'm grateful that we're on the way.
The Tamaki facility that we have, you know, for example, fortunately, we'd made
some early investment in new, um, glasshouse facilities because, uh, we'd
run into a problem in our Tamaki facility in terms of the...
When you have these, um, facilities, you're trying to make sure that whatever
you're testing, nothing can escape from the facility into the wider environment.
And we found we've got a problem in our Tamaki facility, we already had to shift
everything out of there into these glasshouses that we've kind of already got ahead

that's really important, uh, for New Zealanders to realize is that
we are... So much of our economy is dependent on,
um, you know, the biologicals, if you like.
Um, you know, we're largely, you know, our growth comes off, uh,
growing protein and growing fruit, uh, vegetables, and our
forestry sector. And, and for the size of
those, we really need to be able to have a facility that's at the
state-of-the-art level where we can, we can test products.
If we got another, if we got another PSA type outbreak that we had with the
kiwifruit, um, virus, you want to know that we can do all the
sampling, testing and management of that in New Zealand, not send the samples to
Australia. And a really good example of that was with the bird flu outbreak we had
in Dunedin. Because we had rebuilt the lab at
Wallaceville, we were able to do all the sampling and testing in New Zealand, we
didn't have to send it to Australia and get into a long queue of waiting to see
what happens. So it's the same on the plant health side.
That's the other big side of the economy.
And if we get a bark beetle in a forest, uh, or we get
something, uh, something, uh, within our, um, I don't know, we could
get a virus in the tomato sector or in the potato sector or in the onions, we wanna
be able to test those things and check them out.
The importation, the importation side of it's actually the smaller part.
It's about 10% of the total value of that facility.
The big thing is having a laboratory where our people
can contain viruses and diseases and not allow them to
escape, um, and be able to do their important work here in New Zealand.
So, what we do is we underpin, um,
we underpin this $60 billion of export earnings,
uh, by having an animal facility that's, uh, it, it has
the, the most capable laboratories in the country, and at the other

our-

running any number of tests on,
um, things that are picked up in New Zealand, uh, or that are

lot, you know, if you go back to 2010, New Zealand's kiwi, kiwifruit

Uh, we were lucky we had another cultivar that they had invested in, um, through

and we also, we settled, I settled it, $50

And so, uh, it's very, um, it's not an easy
thing- Mm-hmm. ... uh, to manage this balance between...

We thought we'd done all of the right checks and we thought it was okay.
That turned out not to be, uh, and, and
the cost of that then becomes vast.
And so, I think we're just trying to get, we are trying to get that balance a,
bit, a bit more right than where it has been and to try and speed up the
process without compromising. One, one thing that we're
quite keen to, to get, to express to people is
they can share these glasshouses for a lot of varieties.
You don't have to go out on your own.
There are ways to team up to reduce the cost as an importer. Hm.
Um, so y- you know, I think, I think we have to help people with a bit of that
knowledge as well.
Right. Uh, Jo?

team to look, uh, at, particularly at the property level, at the whenua
trust level, around, uh, options for, uh,
assisted natural regeneration and, um, registration of
indigenous, uh, in- in- into the ETS.
So that's an important, uh, opportunity.
Uh, equally, we know there's a lot of, uh, a number of
Maori trusts, uh, are looking very closely at voluntary, um,
networks, um, nature-based credit systems as well.
And, and so Minister Hoger made an announcement in early
a- about, uh, what the government's doing to support, uh,
nature based products and, and that work is underway.
Thank you.
Any further questions?
Just a very quick sup on Cushless.
Oh, yes. Yes.
Um- Thanks.
... tell me, uh, I've heard a few people talk about the potential of
deconstructing the fiber in wood.
Yes.
What's that look like?
Um, the, the humble radiator pine is,
uh, a pretty amazing (laughs) product when you break it down.
And, and so, uh, uh, it's, it's a third cellulose, a
third lignin, and a third other extractives.
So what we've seen, um, and I was fortunate enough to travel to Europe to see this
firsthand, is a growing bioproducts industry where
alongside, uh, industry and manufacturing, like lumber
and, and pulp and paper, uh, processes, there's an opportunity
to, to use processing residue and, and break that down
even further into biochemicals and then use those chemicals to replace
fossil-based chemicals in everyday products, like stains or
adhesives, surfactants. So, uh, there's, there's a
real
awesome opportunity for New Zealand given our fiber-rich position,
uh, to, to leverage more value from the resource that we've
got. And it doesn't involve more planting. We don't need... It, it...
We don't need more resource. It's already there.
Mm-hmm.
So wi- within the construct of a, of an export double
program, we- we're clearly trying to grow new markets and strengthen
existing markets for solid lumber, for logs, and for
engineered products. But, uh, a bioproducts industry is equally
a, a really important growth opportunity.
Uh, and we're looking at closely around how we partner with industry to
create, uh, an environment where we see more investment in that space.
Yep. Yep.
Yeah. I mean, we got a hell of a load of trees.
We do.
(laughs)
And, uh-
We need to do more with them.
Yes. That's right. And, and so that's sort of part of my question because
obviously, um, the value add, the maximizing the
value out of a, any wood or fiber product that we can
is, is the, um, the Holy Grail.
How much, um, investment is there in that depth of
innovation-
Mm-hmm.
... as against how much are we still just a commodity log
provider
to the world and we send a, a raw-
Yeah.
... log away and they turn it into something that's worth a whole lot more money
and sell it back to us or to other people?
Yeah. Uh, I'll start with there's...
It's not perfect and, and there's huge opportunity.
Yeah.
Uh, and as I highlighted, uh, Mike, your question there, uh,
there's, there's opportunity for a public element of
investment to create that, uh, environment for that
innovation. Um,
alongside that-... wood processes, uh,
investing, uh, and that a lot of them do it quietly.
But, um, y- you know, we're seeing a huge shift, I guess, from
kind of mass standardization in the way that we process logs.
But using innovation, using digital technologies,
um, uh, real time response, you know,
servicing a number of markets all around the globe.
So, w- we are seeing a lot of development in that space.
What we're not yet seeing is a, a huge lift yet
in the quantity that's being processed, I'm sure.
So, we're seeing investment, uh, it's improving returns, it's
improving, I guess, productivity and, and, uh, customer
response, but we're not seeing a huge lift in the quantity being
processed. So, there's al- there's always opportunity.
Um, just three weeks ago, so, uh, a business
delegation went to India, uh, and what we're seeing there
is a, a really exciting conversation and opportunity within what we know
is a, is a rapidly growing and large economy, or number of
economies within India. But what's exciting is it's forest growers
and wood processors working together and looking at how collectively can
we enter a new market, and how can we learn from what, what
occurred in China 20, 30 years ago, and how do we, how do we take steps
now so we're not creating a space where, in 15 years' time, it's
just solely a log market? So, it's, it's, it's,
it's complex and it's at all parts of the system, but, uh, a lot of it,
uh, we are sort of starting to see a bit of a change and it, and it, it's coming
down to a bit more coordination.
Follows up on that, just... yeah. So, wh- what is the risk when we're
seeing
mill closures
and that we're losing that capability to add local
value or provide for local markets? Um.
Yeah.
Y- you know, and is, is there a need for the
Parliament to consider more intervention to make sure we don't
lose capability?
Um, first, acknowledging the risk and the downsides.
So, East Valley Sawmill, um, 140
jobs.
Terrible.
That's terrible.
Yeah.
And, and, and that part of the country has had a number of hits in, in different
sectors as well. So, um, when mills close, regional
economies hurt-
Yeah.
... and, and, you know, that's, that's the fact.
Um, the, the other fact though with that closure in East Valley,
............................ is that production, they've invested
significantly into their ............................
Yeah. So, that production's not lost, it's just moved.
But that doesn't help the 140 people clearly.
Right.
Um, so is, is there a need to do more?
Um,
look, uh,
the government can't ... cor- corporates will make decisions, won't
they? Uh, and it's not as though ............................
came, came to us and said, "We need help." They didn't need help.
It, it was a business, it was a planned decision-
Sure.
... uh, in the consolidation that they're, that they're operating with.
Um, there are cases, uh, in Kinleith, for
example, where we're, uh, we are working with, with
OG so that key industrial site there, pulp and paper mill-
Mm-hmm.
... in the middle of the CNI takanoa, uh, we are, we are working
alongside that organization to see if there's anything to, to
support, uh, that, that central government can,
can provide, particularly around energy
security.
Thank you for that. Thank you. Well, um,
there's run out of questions, so-
(laughs)
... see you guys-
See you at 5 o'clock. (laughs)
Yeah. (laughs)
(laughs) Speaking with
enthusiasm. (laughs)
Yeah, Ray, you should, you should be a lot more
enthusiastic. (laughs)
(laughs) Coolness galore. Yeah. Ready for a life.
No, thank you very much for your time and, um,
for the answers you've given, and I'm sure there'll be pos- possibly a few
written questions that follow up, um,
from the committee. That's, um-
That's tough stuff (laughs)
Very good. Thank you very much.
(laughs) Very good. Thank you.
Thank
you. Thank you.
